title: The Message Lizard
desc: The message-carrying lizard escaped. It has sensitive documents strapped to it.

steps.text:
Message lizards are trained from hatching to carry sealed documents between Mipedian outposts. They're fast, they're reliable, and they're nearly impossible to catch once they decide to go off-route.

This one — a speckled blue runner named Kik — decided that today's message could wait while she pursued a particularly attractive sand beetle across the Sha-Kree Flats. The message strapped to her back contains diplomatic correspondence meant for the royal court. It's sealed, but the seal won't survive Kik's enthusiasm for rolling in sand.

The handler is already running. "She always goes east! Cut her off at the rock formation!"

You can see Kik — a blue streak across the sand, zigging and zagging after invisible prey, the message canister bouncing on her harness.

options.label: Sprint east and cut her off
Use bait — lizards can't resist dried beetles

steps.text:
You run east, angling to intercept. Kik is fast but predictable — she follows beetle trails, which curve in wide arcs across the sand. You take the straight line.

The rock formation is a cluster of sandstone pillars — Kik's favorite sunning spot, according to the handler. If she's chasing beetles, she'll eventually circle back here. You crouch behind a pillar and wait.

Five minutes. Ten. Then a skittering sound — claws on stone. Kik scrambles up the opposite side of your pillar, message canister intact, a sand beetle clamped proudly in her jaws.

She sees you. You see her. There's a moment of mutual assessment.

You move slowly. Kik's eyes track your hand. You reach for the harness strap. She bolts — but you've positioned yourself between her and the open sand. She runs into the gap between two pillars, realizes it's a dead end, and turns to face you with the resigned dignity of a caught creature.

You clip the lead to her harness. The message canister is sandy but intact, the seal unbroken. Kik eats her beetle with an air of injured pride.

The handler arrives, panting. "Got her. Good." She takes the lead. "This is the third time this month. I keep telling the court — use hawk-pigeons. They don't chase beetles." She looks at Kik, who is now trying to dig a beetle out of the sand while still on the lead. "But the court likes tradition."

options.label: Help deliver the message to its destination

steps.text:
You have dried beetles in your pack — trail rations that double as universal reptile bribery. You crack one open and wave it in the air. The scent carries on the desert breeze.

Kik stops mid-chase. Her head swivels. Her nostrils flare. The sand beetle she was pursuing — alive, fast, barely worth the calories — cannot compete with a dried beetle's concentrated aroma.

She turns. She approaches. She does the thing that all message lizards do when they want something — she sits up on her hind legs, front claws tucked, tail straight, looking as dignified as possible. The trained "delivery complete" pose, repurposed for begging.

You hold the beetle just out of reach. Kik stretches. You clip the lead to her harness with your other hand. Then you give her the beetle.

She crunches it with the satisfaction of a creature who has gotten exactly what she wanted. The message canister is undamaged. The handler catches up and takes the lead.

"Beetles. Every time. I should just carry a pocket full." She pauses. "Actually, last time I did that, she smelled them on me and followed me home instead of delivering the message."

Kik looks at your pack, clearly aware that more beetles exist. You give her one more for the road. The handler sighs. "Great. Now she likes you more than me."

The diplomatic correspondence reaches the royal court on time, if slightly sandy.
